title: [YouTube] Fix 403 error and throttling
body: Decrypting `signatureCipher` to fix the recent 403 error, as well as to solve the long-standing download speed throttling issue (#2950). This was made possible thanks to the lightweight JavaScript interpreter dukpy.

step_plan.summary: Fix YouTube 403 errors and download throttling by correctly handling `signatureCipher` (including `sp`) and by executing YouTube player JavaScript via dukpy to decipher signatures and transform throttling parameters like `n`. Update packaging to include the dukpy dependency.

steps.operation: Parse `signatureCipher`/`cipher` into `url`, `s`, and `sp` and persist these fields per stream entry; also surface any throttling-related query params (e.g., `n`) found in the ciphered URL so they can be rewritten later
steps.reason: Recent YouTube responses frequently provide stream URLs only via `signatureCipher`. Correct final URL construction requires both the ciphered signature (`s`) and the signature parameter name (`sp`). Capturing throttling-related parameters early ensures all stream variants (formats + adaptiveFormats) can be fixed consistently.
steps.side_effects: Changes the internal stream dict/schema consumed by `extract()`; any code assuming only a plain `url` may need to tolerate `s`/`sp` presence. May affect DASH/adaptive stream enumeration; requires sanity checks across progressive/adaptive formats.

steps.operation: Finalize download URLs by (1) deciphering `s` and appending it using the exact `sp` key (fallback only when missing), and (2) applying throttling parameter (`n`) transformation before returning the URL (ensure existing `ratebypass=yes` behavior is preserved where applicable)
steps.reason: The 403s are commonly caused by appending the deciphered signature under the wrong query key (hardcoded `sig`). Throttling is often enforced via an `n` query parameter that must be transformed using logic from the same player JS; updating it here ensures the final URL is valid and performant right before download.
steps.side_effects: Alters the exact query string generated for streams; could impact caching and URL comparisons. May require fetching/caching player JS once per video to avoid performance regression. Should be validated against playlist flows and any code paths that reuse extracted URLs.

steps.operation: Replace/augment the fragile regex-based JS-to-Python `s_to_sig` decipher with a dukpy-backed JS evaluation path: extract the decipher function (and helper object) from the player JS, execute it in dukpy to compute the signature; also expose a similar JS execution hook usable for `n` transformation
steps.reason: YouTube frequently changes the player JS structure, breaking regex-based transpilation and causing invalid signatures (403). Using dukpy to execute the real JS logic makes deciphering resilient. The same mechanism can be reused to compute the throttling `n` transform to address download speed throttling.
steps.side_effects: Introduces a runtime dependency on dukpy for YouTube extraction; failures to import dukpy must be handled (either hard error with guidance or fallback to old method). JS extraction/parsing mistakes can break all YouTube downloads, so add defensive parsing and caching. Execution of remote JS increases attack surface; ensure only minimal required functions are evaluated.

steps.operation: Declare `dukpy` as a dependency in packaging metadata (adjust `install_requires`/equivalent around setup configuration so YouTube functionality can rely on it)
steps.reason: The PR’s YouTube fixes depend on dukpy at runtime; without declaring the dependency, installs from PyPI/source will continue to fail at decipher time.
steps.side_effects: May increase installation footprint and add platform constraints depending on dukpy availability. If made mandatory, it affects all users; if made optional, additional runtime checks and error messaging are required. Verify packaging still works for editable installs and distribution builds.
